docs.title: Tharun's Lab
docs.text:
This is my public learning log.

My main site covers my professional work as a Technical Writer. This site is different — it's where I document things as I build and learn them, unpolished and in progress.

docs.title: What you'll find here
docs.text:
• Experiments — AI tools and ideas I'm exploring, mostly applied to documentation
  • TIL — Short notes on things I learned recently
  • Now — What I'm currently focused on

docs.title: Who I am
docs.text:
Technical Writer at Siemens, based in Bangalore. I work at the intersection of documentation, AI tooling, and developer experience. Lately I've been building tools that make documentation smarter — not just prettier.

Last updated: March 2026

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
AI tools, ideas, and builds — mostly applied to technical writing and documentation.

These are works in progress. Some will ship. Some won't. All of them taught me something.

Experiment What it is Status Doc-Scanner AI-powered documentation analysis tool Active RAG with ChromaDB Style rule retrieval using vector search Active Agent Layer LLM function-calling without frameworks Exploring

docs.title: Agent Layer
docs.text: Exploring how to build an AI agent on top of Doc-Scanner using raw LLM function-calling APIs — without frameworks like LangChain or LlamaIndex.

docs.title: The idea
docs.text:
Instead of a single LLM call per document, an agent could:

  1. Decide which analysis to run based on the document type
  2. Call the right tools — readability check, style rule retrieval, rewrite
  3. Synthesize results into a single structured report

docs.title: Why raw APIs
docs.text: Frameworks abstract away the function-calling loop, which makes debugging harder and customization limited. Building from raw APIs means I understand exactly what's happening at each step.

docs.title: Current status
docs.text: Early exploration. The function definitions are drafted. The orchestration loop is next.

docs.title: Open question
docs.text: How many tool calls is too many before latency becomes a problem for a documentation review workflow?

docs.title: Doc-Scanner
docs.text: An AI-powered documentation analysis tool I built to solve real problems in my own writing workflow.

docs.title: The problem it solves
docs.text:
Style guide enforcement is manual and inconsistent. Readability checks happen too late. Feedback loops between writers and reviewers are slow.

Doc-Scanner automates this — analyzing documents against style rules, measuring readability, and suggesting rewrites in real time.

docs.title: Stack
docs.text:
• Python + Flask — backend and blueprint architecture
  • ChromaDB — vector storage for style rules
  • RAG — retrieves relevant rules per document segment
  • spaCy — NLP for sentence analysis and rewriting
  • Readability metrics — Flesch-Kincaid and similar

docs.title: What I learned
docs.text:
• How to structure a Flask app with dynamic rule loading via blueprints
  • RAG works best when your knowledge base is well-structured — the retrieval is only as good as what you put in
  • Raw LLM function-calling APIs give you more control than LangChain, at the cost of more setup

docs.title: Current focus
docs.text: Productizing this end-to-end — proper UI, exportable reports, and a deployable version others can actually use.

docs.title: Key decision
docs.text: I deliberately chose raw LLM APIs over LangChain. The abstraction LangChain provides wasn't worth the loss of control for what I needed.

docs.title: RAG with ChromaDB
docs.text: Notes on using Retrieval-Augmented Generation to store and retrieve writing style rules inside Doc-Scanner.

docs.title: Why RAG for style rules
docs.text: A style guide has hundreds of rules. Sending all of them to an LLM every time is expensive, slow, and noisy. RAG lets me retrieve only the rules relevant to the current piece of content.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1. Style rules are chunked and embedded into ChromaDB as vectors
  2. When a document segment is analyzed, similar rules are retrieved by cosine similarity
  3. Only those rules are passed to the LLM with the content
  4. The LLM flags issues and suggests rewrites based on the retrieved rules

docs.title: What surprised me
docs.text: The quality of retrieval depends heavily on how you write the rules themselves. Vague rules retrieve poorly. Specific, example-driven rules retrieve well.

docs.title: What's next
docs.text: Experimenting with rule weighting — giving higher priority to critical style violations over minor preferences.

docs.title: TIL — Today I Learned
docs.text: Short notes on things I picked up recently. No polish, just signal.

docs.title: March 2026
docs.text:
MkDocs gh-deploy handles everything You don't need to manually manage the gh-pages branch. mkdocs gh-deploy builds, branches, and pushes in one command. Wish I'd known this earlier.

ChromaDB retrieval quality depends on input quality Spent an hour debugging poor RAG results. The embeddings were fine. The style rules I'd written were just too vague to retrieve meaningfully. Rewrote them to be specific and example-driven — retrieval improved immediately.

PowerShell doesn't use backticks as code fences Triple backticks are a Markdown convention, not a shell command. Learned this the hard way.

Add a new entry whenever you learn something worth remembering.

docs.title: Technical Writing Work
docs.text: My documentation work spans user manuals, API docs, and internal tooling guides — delivered through a docs-as-code workflow using MkDocs and Fluid Topics.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
• Write in Markdown, review in VS Code
  • Publish via MkDocs to Fluid Topics portal
  • Follow Program Increment (PI) release cycles — docs ship in the same sprint as features

docs.title: Areas
docs.text:
• User manuals for software products
  • API documentation
  • Internal automation and tooling documentation
